# Legal accuracy and court-document rules

Last reviewed: 17 July 2026

This reference is a dated summary, not the primary source. Check the current rule or practice note before relying on it where a document will be filed, served, tendered, or used in proceedings. The Supreme Court of NSW page for Practice Note SC Gen 23 is https://supremecourt.nsw.gov.au/practice-procedure/generative-artificial-intelligence.html.

## General rules, all jurisdictions

- Never invent an authority, quotation, pinpoint, statutory provision, evidentiary reference, or outcome.
- Never state that a source has been verified unless it was actually checked against the primary source.
- Preserve a citation the user supplies during editing, but do not treat it as verified merely because the user supplied it.
- If a primary source is available and verification is asked for, check the primary source.
- If verification cannot be completed, record the item as outstanding outside the proposed filed text rather than asserting it is confirmed.
- Do not place confidential, privileged, suppressed, subpoenaed, or otherwise restricted material into an external search or tool.
- Do not make an external network call containing client or matter information without express authority and an appropriate controlled environment.
- Distinguish drafting assistance from any representation that a practitioner has discharged a personal professional obligation.
- Identify the applicable court or tribunal rule before applying a jurisdiction-specific restriction. Do not apply one court's rule as though it were national law.

## Practice Note SC Gen 23 (Supreme Court of NSW)

This Practice Note applies to proceedings in the Supreme Court of NSW. It is not a universal Australian rule, and other courts and tribunals have their own rules. Summary of the version issued 28 January 2025, which commenced 3 February 2025:

- Paragraph 6: technology that merely corrects spelling or grammar, provides transcription or translation, assists with formatting, or otherwise does not generate substantive content is not treated as Gen AI for the Practice Note. Ordinary search engines and dedicated legal research software are also outside it.
- Paragraph 9A: material subject to a non-publication or suppression order, the implied (Harman) undertaking, production on subpoena, or a statutory publication prohibition must not be entered into any Gen AI program unless the responsible person is satisfied that the information will stay within the platform's controlled, confidentiality-restricted environment, will be used only for that proceeding, and will not be used to train any model.
- Paragraph 9B: subject to 9A, Gen AI may be used to generate chronologies, indexes, and witness lists; to prepare briefs or draft Crown Case Statements; to summarise or review documents and transcripts; and to prepare written submissions or summaries of argument, subject to paragraphs 16 to 18.
- Paragraph 10: Gen AI must not be used to generate the content of affidavits, witness statements, character references, or other material intended to reflect a deponent's or witness's evidence or opinion, or material tendered in evidence or used in cross-examination. Work merely preparatory to drafting that evidence is not prohibited.
- Paragraph 11: this material must contain and reflect the person's own knowledge, not AI-generated content.
- Paragraph 12: Gen AI must not be used to alter, embellish, strengthen, dilute, or otherwise rephrase a witness's evidence expressed in written form.
- Paragraphs 13 to 15: an affidavit, witness statement, or character reference must carry a disclosure that Gen AI was not used to generate its content; in exceptional cases leave may be sought to use Gen AI for an annexure or exhibit, and the application must identify the proposed use, the program and version, whether it is closed or open source and its privacy settings, and the benefit.
- Paragraphs 16 to 18: where Gen AI is used to prepare written submissions or summaries of argument, the author must verify in the body that all citations and authorities exist, are accurate, and are relevant, and must similarly verify references to evidence; that verification must not be carried out solely by a Gen AI tool; and use of Gen AI does not absolve the author of professional and ethical obligations.
- Paragraphs 19 to 24: an expert report must state the expert's opinion and reasoning; Gen AI must not be used to draft or prepare the content of an expert report, or any part of it, without prior leave of the Court, subject to paragraph 23; a leave application must identify the proposed use, program and version, source type and privacy settings, benefit, and any documents to be submitted; where leave is granted the expert must disclose which parts used Gen AI and keep a record of how it was used; in professional negligence claims under UCPR r 31.36 and certain workplace injury damages matters, leave must be sought at the first directions hearing; and practitioners must draw these requirements to the expert's attention.
- Paragraph 26: the Practice Note is reviewed periodically, so confirm the current version before relying on this summary.

## Working with NSW Supreme Court material

Affidavits, witness statements, and character references:

- Do not generate or rephrase the substantive evidence or opinion.
- Preparatory assistance is permitted: organising source documents, building a chronology, identifying the topics the evidence should cover, drafting non-leading questions to put to the witness, or checking internal consistency.
- Proofreading confined to spelling, grammar, and formatting sits outside the Practice Note under paragraph 6, but it must not change meaning, emphasis, certainty, sequence, or the witness's own language.
- The document must carry the paragraph 13 disclosure that Gen AI was not used to generate its content.

Written submissions and summaries of argument:

- Drafting assistance is permitted.
- Do not invent a citation and do not silently repair one.
- Do not insert a statement that verification has occurred unless the required human verification has actually been done.
- Where verification is outstanding, note it outside the draft rather than asserting confirmation inside it.
- Every reference to evidence must trace to a supplied affidavit, exhibit, or transcript.

Expert reports:

- Do not draft the substantive content of an expert report for a Supreme Court of NSW proceeding unless the user confirms prior leave and its terms.
- Preserve the expert's opinion and reasoning as the expert's own work.
- Where leave exists, apply the disclosure and record-keeping requirements.

## Verification is a human obligation

A citation or evidentiary reference is not confirmed until a person has checked it against the primary source. Under SC Gen 23 that verification must not be carried out solely by AI, and using Gen AI does not discharge the practitioner's professional obligations. Where a document will be filed, served, tendered, or relied on, the practitioner remains responsible for confirming every authority and every reference.
